title: The sweet spot of a separation column
author: OpenDiscover
blurb: Every chromatography column — the workhorse that separates the molecules in a blood sample, a drug batch, or a river-water pollutant panel — has one best speed. The van Deemter equation H = A + B/u + C·u splits peak broadening into three causes: eddy diffusion (A, a fixed floor), longitudinal diffusion (B/u, worst when you run slowly and give molecules time to spread), and resistance to mass transfer (C·u, worst when you run fast). The two speed-dependent terms fight, so the plate height bottoms out at u_opt = √(B/C) ≈ 6.3 mm/s here — the fastest run that still gives the sharpest possible peaks. This run sits the velocity right at that minimum: efficiency versus optimum reads 1.0, and the plotted curve shows the characteristic U you are balancing on top of. Go faster to save time and you pay in resolution; go slower and diffusion quietly ruins the separation anyway.
